FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


March 2, 2010


Thomas Guarino
Siskiyou County Counsel
P.O. Box 659
205 Lane Street
Yreka, California 96097

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-027

Dear Mr. Guarino:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Shasta Valley Resource Conservation District (the SVRCD) Directors Richard Kuck, Don Meamber, and Richard Lemos and Siskiyou Resource Conservation District (the SRCD) Directors William P. Krum, David A. Black, Richard G. Barnes, Greg Farnam, and John P. Spencer regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Although you wrote in two letters requesting advice, one on behalf of each agency, since the facts are substantially the same, we have merged the two requests into this single response.

Please note that our advice is based solely on provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Also note our advice is based solely on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)


___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The Regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 2

QUESTIONS

1. Do the three SVRCD directors (Directors Kuck, Meamber, and Lemos) and five
SRCD directors (Directors Krum, Black, Barnes, Farnam, and Spencer) have disqualifying
conflicts of interest in voting on the Watershed-wide Permitting Program (the "program")
Documents?

2. Does the "exceptional circumstances" exception in Regulation 18707 apply to allow
the disqualified members to vote despite the conflict of interest?

3. If the three SVRCD and five SRCD directors are determined to be disqualified from
voting, will any of the directors be legally required to participate?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The three SVRCD (Directors Kuck, Meamber, and Lemos) and four of the five SRCD
directors (Directors Krum, Barnes, Farnam, and Spencer) are disqualified from voting to approve
the program documents if there will be a material financial effect on their economic interests, as
described below. SRCD Director Black does not appear to have a conflict of interest under your
facts.

2. The public generally exception, including the "exceptional circumstances" provision,
does not apply.

3. Assuming that either the SVRCD or SRCD cannot reach a quorum due to the conflicts
of interest, then the rule of legally required participation applies.

FACTS

The Department of Fish and Game (the "Department") regulates agricultural water
diversions in the Shasta River and Scott River watersheds. Under Fish and Game rules, it is
unlawful for any person to divert or obstruct the natural flow of the rivers without first notifying
the Department and obtaining an Incidental Take Permits and Streambed Alteration Agreement.
Currently, agricultural diverters must go through the standard permitting process to comply with
the rule. This standard process includes complying with CEQA. You stated that the cost to
prepare environmental documents for the permit and agreement is between $10,000 and $20,000
each; and the filing fees for CEQA documents are between approximately $1,993 and $2,766.

The Department, the SRCD, and SVRCD staff have developed watershed-wide
permitting programs for agricultural diverters in the Shasta and Scott River watersheds. Under
this plan, rather than individually obtaining a permit and agreement, the Department issues a
watershed-wide "master permit" to each resource conservation district. Agricultural operators
will be able to apply for sub-permits under the master permit. In addition, agricultural operators
who obtain subpermits to the master permit may use a streamlined individual streambed

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 3

alteration agreement process. The agricultural operators under the proposed permitting program
will not be required to prepare separate environmental documents and will avoid these costs.

The master permit program requires the respective districts to take on program
administration duties, educational tasks, monitoring, and to submit a $100,000 letter of credit to
the Department, which may be drawn upon if a subpermittee fails to comply with the
requirements of the sub-permit.² The SVRCD and SRCD are considering the master plan
program.

The Shasta Valley Resource Conservation District: You stated that the SVRCD is
composed of five directors, three of whom will have to acquire a permit or are financially
associated with an entity that will have to acquire a permit.

• Board President, Director Richard Kuck: Richard Kuck has an interest in the Kuck Hereford
Ranch. Mr. Kuck is a partner in and operator of the Kuck Hereford Ranch, which will obtain
a permit under the Permitting Program if it is approved. The Kuck Hereford Ranch is also a
source of income for Mr. Kuck.

• Director Don Meamber: Director Don Meamber is the sole proprietor Meamber Ranch,
which will obtain a permit under the program if it is approved. Mr. Meamber reports that he
has a direct investment worth $2,000 or greater in his ranching business and a direct
investment of $2,000 or more in the fair market value of the ranch property. The cattle ranch
is a source of income for Mr. Meamber in an amount greater than $500 in the last 12 period.
The ranching operation's net income in the most recent fiscal year was less than $750,000. If
subject to normal permitting procedures, Mr. Meamber believes the cost of obtaining a
permit would decrease his personal income.

• Director Richard Lemos: Director Richard Lemos owns ranch property that receives water
from the Shasta River Water Association (the "Association") and will not personally be
subject to the program requirements. However, the Association he receives water from
would acquire permit under the program. It is possible that if the program were not approved,
then the Association could pass the cost of obtaining a sub-permit under normal permitting
procedures on to water users such as Mr. Lemos in the form of increased fees. In addition,
Mr. Lemos is on the board of the Association, which is a 501(c)(12) corporation. Mr. Lemos
is also employed by this Association and receives income of more than $500 in the last 12-
month period.

The Siskiyou Resource Conservation District: You stated that the SRCD is composed
of five directors, all of whom will have to acquire a permit or are financially associated with an
entity that will have to acquire a permit.

• Board President, Director William P. Krum: Director Krum has an interest in the Broken K.
Ranch, L.P (greater than $2,000 investment and an interest in real property owned by the

________________________________
² The Department may also draw upon the letter of credit if the district fails to fulfill its permit obligations.

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 4

ranch worth more than $2,000). Mr. Krum also holds a position of management with Broken
K. Ranch. The Broken K. Ranch's net income in the most recent fiscal year was less than
$750,000. Mr. Krum is a co-trustee and co-beneficiary of the Bill and Narda Family Trust.
Mr. Krum receives income from the trust and through his status as a beneficiary of the Trust,
the ranch is a source of income to Mr. Krum in an amount greater than $500 in the last 12
months. The Bill and Narda Family Trust is the general partner of the Broken K. Ranch.
Broken K. Ranch will need to obtain a permit under the program. The Bill and Narda Family
Trust also receives income from the Scott River Water Trust. The Master Permit dictates that
a Scott River Water Trust must be developed as part of the permitting program.

• Director David A. Black: Director Black owns ranch property that receives water from the
irrigation district. Mr. Black will not personally be subject to the permitting program.
However, the irrigation district he receives water from would be subject to the program.

• Director Richard G. Barnes: Director Barnes is the sole proprietor of the Limerick Ranch
and in which he holds an investment of $2,000 or more and an ownership interest in the
ranch's real property valued at $2,000 or more. Limerick Ranch will obtain a permit under
the permitting program.

• Director Greg Farnam: Director Farnam is the sole proprietor of a farm and reports. He
has an investment worth $2,000 or greater in the farm and an ownership interest of $2,000 or
more in the fair market value of the farming property. The farm's net income in the most
recent fiscal year was less than $750,000. The farm will obtain a permit under the program.

• Director John P. Spencer: Director Spencer owns the Spencer Ranch Company with his
wife. As a beneficiary of the John and Carol Family Trust, Mr. Spencer has an investment of
$2,000 or more in the ranch company an interest in the property on which the ranch operates
in the amount of $2,000 or more. This trust owns the ranch property. The Spencer Ranch
Company's net income in the most recent fiscal year was less than $750,000. Mr. Spencer
additionally reported being a member of Farmer's Ditch. It is not known whether Farmer's
Ditch would subject to the program.

You also noted:

• The SVRCD estimates that approximately 70 to 75 superpermits will be issued under the
Master Permit.

• A 2007 County Business Pattern study released in August of 2009 indicates that the total
number of businesses in the County of Siskiyou is 1,277 and of that number 40 are
categorized as agriculture, forestry, fishing and hunting," which is just over 3% of all
businesses. You stated it is unlikely that agricultural businesses requiring permits will
constitute 2,000 or 25% or greater of the businesses in the district.

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 5

• According to the Siskiyou County Administrator's Office, there are 4,038,843 acres in
Siskiyou County. Of that figure, 1,153,246 acres are farmland and 2,525,216 are
rangeland/woodland. Only 12,381 acres are urban.

ANALYSIS

The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that
"[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons
who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of
the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 requires analysis
of the questions outlined below.

Steps One and Two: Are the individuals in question "public officials" and will they be
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency. Regulation 18701 provides:

"(a) For purposes of Government Code section 82048, which defines
'public official,' and Government Code section 82019, which defines 'designated
employee," the following definitions apply:

"(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried
members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking authority. "

You stated that both the SVRCD and SRCD Boards will be asked to make decisions
concerning the Department of Fish and Game Incidental Take Permit or the Streambed
Alteration Agreement Memorandum of Understanding and Master List of Terms and Conditions.
Thus, the members of the boards will be making decisions. Both Boards have decisionmaking
authority. Thus, all the directors are public officials subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).)

You have asked whether the directors can vote on the permit program. The directors will
"make a governmental decision" if they vote on the Department of Fish and Game Incidental
Take Permit or the Streambed Alteration Agreement Memorandum of Understanding and Master
List of Terms and Conditions. (Regulations 18702 - 18702.3.)

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 6

Step Three: What are the directors' economic interests — the possible sources of a conflict of interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).

Shasta Valley Resource Conservation District

Director Kuck: Director Kuck is a partner and operator of Kuck Hereford Ranch. We assume his interest is worth more than $2,000. Mr. Kuck also owns the ranch property.

Director Meamber: Director Meamber conducts ranching operations as a sole proprietor and has an investment of more than $2,000 in the business, Meamber Ranch. He has an investment interest in the business of $2,000, and an interest in the real property in excess of $2,000.

Director Lemos: Director Lemos owns property in the region in which he presumably has an interest of more than $2,000. He also is a director on the board of and receives income from the Shasta River Water Association. The Shasta River Water Association is a 501(c)(12) nonprofit.

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 7

Moreover, Directors Kuck and Member each receive income from their respective
businesses.³

Siskiyou Resource Conservation District

Board President, Director William P. Krum: Director Krum has an interest in the
Broken K. Ranch, L.P (an investment worth $2,000 or more and an interest in real property
owned by the ranch). Mr. Krum also holds a position of management in the Broken K. Ranch.
Mr. Krum is a co-trustee and co-beneficiary of the Bill and Narda Family Trust. The trust is the
general partner of the Broken K. Ranch. Mr. Krum receives income from the trust and through
his status as a beneficiary of the trust, the ranch is a source of income to Mr. Krum in an amount
greater than $500 in the last 12 months.

Section 87103 provides that an official has a pro-rata interest in any investment or other
interest owned by a trust, and sources of income to the trust, if the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or
greater. Regulation 18234 further provides that a person has a direct, indirect or beneficial
interest in a trust if the person is:

"(c) For purposes of determining whether an official has an economic
interest in interests in real property, sources of income, and investments of a trust,
the official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest in a trust if the official is:

"(1) A trustor and:

"(A) Can revoke or terminate the trust;

"(B) Has retained or reserved any rights to the income or principal of the
trust, or retained any reversionary or remainder interest; or

"(C) Has retained or reserved any power of appointment, including but not
limited to the power to change the trustee, or the power to amend, alter or
designate, either alone or in conjunction with anyone else, the person or persons
who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or income.

"(2) A beneficiary and:

"(A) Presently receives income; or

"(B) Has an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. For
purposes of this subsection, an individual has an irrevocable future right to

³ Directors Kuck and Meamber also may have customers of clients who are sources of income, however, we do not
have any facts to suggest these clients or customers will be affected by the decisions in question.

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 8

receive income or principal if the trust is irrevocable, unless one of the following
applies:

"(i) Powers exist to consume, invade, or appoint the principal for the
benefit of beneficiaries other than the official and such powers are not limited by
an ascertainable standard relating to the health, education, support, or
maintenance of the beneficiaries; or

"(ii) Under the terms of the trust, someone other than the official can
designate the persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or income."

Finally, the Bill and Narda Family Trust also receives income from the Scott River Water
Trust. Thus, Scott River Water Trust is also a source of income.

Director David A. Black: Director Black owns ranch property.

Director Richard G. Barnes: Director Barnes is the sole proprietor of the Limerick
Ranch and reports an investment of $2,000 or more in Limerick Ranch and an interest in the
ranch's real property valued at $2,000 or more.

Director Greg Farnam: Director Farnam is the sole proprietor of a farming operation
and reports that he has an investment worth $2,000 or greater in the business and an interest of
$2,000 or more in the fair market value of the farming property. The farming operation's net
income in the most recent fiscal year was less than $750,000.

Director John P. Spencer: Director Spencer owns the Spencer Ranch Company with
his wife. Mr. Spencer has an investment of $2,000 or more in the ranch company an interest in
the property on which the ranch operates in the amount of $2,000 or more as a beneficiary of the
John and Carol Family Trust. This trust owns the ranch property. The Spencer Ranch
Company's net income in the most recent fiscal year was less than $750,000.

Mr. Spencer additionally reported being a member of Farmer's Ditch. It is not known
whether Farmer's Ditch would subject to the program.

Personal Finances: You also stated that all the members of both boards have an
economic interest in their personal finances and those of their immediate family. As stated in
Regulation 18703.5, a governmental decision will have a personal financial effect "if the
decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or
her immediate family increasing or decreasing." However, Regulation 18705.5(a) also provides:

"When determining whether a governmental decision has a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her personal
finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or
indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 9

value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct
or indirect investment interest shall be considered."

Under your facts, all of the directors in question would have potential conflicts of
interests based on the following:

(1) They would incur (or avoid) permitting costs on behalf of businesses they own or
have an interest in due to income received or by holding a business position with the business.⁴

(2) There may be affects on the fair market value of the property they own.

(3) Similarly, the potential permit obligation with respect to Director Lemos will be
incurred by the nonprofit employer of Director Lemos, not him personally.

Since the financial effects will be analyzed under the business interest and real property
rules, their "personal finances" will not be considered.³ Thus, we do not further analyze the
financial effects as effects on the personal finances of the officials.

Step Four: Are the directors' economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?

Business interests and Sources of Income: Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business
entity or source of income is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency when
that business entity or source of income, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."

None of the businesses or sources of income in question are applicants with respect to the
decisions in question, nor would they be considered the subject of the decision.

⁴ Section 82005 of the Act defines "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including
but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or
association.
³ While it is indeed true that owners of businesses in many cases will be impacted personally if their businesses are
financially impacted (such as Director Krum needing to add capital to the business or the other directors who
operate their businesses as sole proprietors), the intent of the language in Regulation 18705.5(a) was to avoid
reanalyzing the same financial effect twice -- first under a $20,000 materiality threshold for the official's business,
and then at a $250 threshold for personal financial effects. Absent the limiting language, the lower materiality
threshold of the personal financial effects rule would swallow the materiality standards applicable to businesses and
real property.

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 10


Real Property: With respect to real property, Regulation 18704.2 provides:

"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is
directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest or, any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental decision.
For purposes of subdivision (a)(5), real property is located "within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of
the governmental decision" if any part of the real property is within 500 feet of
the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment project area.

"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion
from any city, county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real
property in which the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the
real property. For purposes of this subdivision, the terms "zoning" and
"rezoning" shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land use
designation on the real property in which the official has an interest.

"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses
of the real property in which the official, has an interest.

"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in
which the official has an interest.

"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of it
is located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment area.

"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets,
water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which
the official has an interest will receive new or improved services."

None of the interests you described appears to be directly involved in the decisions in
question. Thus, the interests would all be considered indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).)

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 11

Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met?

The next step is to identify the materiality standards that apply and whether it is foreseeable the standard will be met. Regulation 18706 provides:

"(a) A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, within the meaning of Government Code section 87103, if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards (see Cal. Code Regs., tit. 2, §§ 18704, 18705) applicable to that economic interest will be met as a result of the governmental decision."

"[T]he question of whether financial consequences upon a business entity are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made must always depend on the facts of each particular case." (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) In each case, the official must look at the facts surrounding each decision, and the nature of the economic interest to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision is substantially likely to cause a material financial effect as set forth above.

The Commission is not a finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because the determination of foreseeability and materiality is necessarily a factual question, we must leave this ultimate determination to you. If it is reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard for any of your economic interests will be met, then you will be prohibited from participating in the specific plan update decisions.

Business Entities: Regulation 18705.1(c) provides for a business that in the most recent fiscal year had net income of less than $750,000 (such as the businesses of SVRCD Director Member and Kuck, and the businesses of SRCD Directors Krum, Farnum, and Spencer),6 the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

6 We did not have the financial size of the ranches of Director Barnes and Black businesses, but assume that the net income of these businesses in the most recent fiscal year is less than $750,000. If the net income is higher, higher thresholds apply in Regulation 18705.1(c).

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 12

You noted that the associated expense of applying for a permit without the master permit plan in place could be as high as $20,000. The Commission cannot act as a finder of fact, but if it is reasonably foreseeable that the directors businesses will incur or reduce expenses by more than $5,000 in a fiscal year, the financial effect is considered material.

You stated that SRCD Director Black's business would not be subject to the program. Thus, under the regulation the effects would not be considered material. However, you noted that his business expenses might still increase if the irrigation district that provides him water has to obtain a permit and increases its rates. However, based on the limited facts before us, this financial effect seems speculative at best. The irrigation district would have to make the independent determination to obtain a permit, to recover some or all of the costs through rate increases, and these rate increases would have to equal $5,000 or more on Director Black's business. Thus, it does not appear foreseeable that Director Black's business will be materially affected or that he will have a conflict of interest. However, we must leave this ultimate factual determination to you.

Nonprofits: Director Lemos has an interest in a nonprofit, the Shasta River Water Association, as a source of income. We do not know the size of the Shasta River Water Association. For a relatively small nonprofit, Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(F) provides:

"For an entity whose gross annual receipts are $100,000 or less, the effect of the decision will be any of the following:

"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.

"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more.

"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more."

Again, in light of your estimate of the financial expense of a permit absent the master permit program, the financial effect on the expenses of the Association would be material.

Real Property: Regulation 18705.2(b) provides for indirectly involved real property interests.

"The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 13

the real property in which the public official has an interest. Examples of specific
circumstances that will be considered include, but are not limited to,
circumstances where the decision affects:

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;

"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;

"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."

The material financial effects of the decisions on the fair market value of the property on
which the directors operate their farms and ranches is a bit more speculative. Presumably, the
cost of the permit can affect the amount that a buyer might pay to purchase the property. In any
case, the potential materiality of this financial effect and whether the presumption of no
materiality will be rebutted is a factual determination that we must leave to you and the officials
in question.

Step Seven: "Public Generally" Exception

You have also asked if we determine the board members have a conflict of interest under
the Act, may they nevertheless participate in the decision under the "public generally exception."
(Section 87103, Regulation 18707.) Regulation 18707.1 provides the general rule for applying
the public generally exception -- the decision must affect a (1) significant segment of the public
generally in substantially the same manner as it affects the public official. For real property
interests, the "significant segment" is defined as 10 percent of all real property owners, or all
residential property owners, in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official
represents or 5,000 real property owners, or residential property owners, in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency. For business interests, the public generally threshold is even higher -- 25
percent or more or 2,000 business entities operating within the City.

You have indicated that the number of potential sub-permittees under the permitting
program would not meet the thresholds set forth above for a "significant segment."

You also reference the "exceptional circumstances" exception. This is an exception to
situations where, while the segment of the population affected is less than stated in the other
provisions of the regulation, due to exceptional circumstances, the population affected by the
decision is a "significant" segment and the public generally exception applies. (Regulation
18707.1 (b).) This provision has been interpreted very narrowly. "This exception was intended
for application in circumstances qualitatively different from the norm when the decisions, for
example, might involve measures taken in response to natural disasters." (Jackson Advice
Letter, No. A-96-338.) It was adopted in conjunction with an exception applicable to states of

Our File No. A-10-027
Page 14

emergency (Regulation 18707.6), both of which generally apply where large numbers of persons
are affected.

While the potential consequences of inaction if the board is unable to act do appear
significant, it will not be a direct effect of the disqualifications in question. Not only can
operators act individually to comply with the new rules, but the Department also has discretion

Section 87101 allows an official, who is otherwise disqualified to participate in a
governmental decision, if the official's participation is "legally required." (Section 87101;

impossible for the decision to be made without the participation of a disqualified official, and
where there is no "alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the
statute authorizing the decision." (Regulation 18708(a), (c), and (d); In re Tobias (1999) 13
FPPC Ops. 5.) Typically this exception is invoked when, due to disqualification, an agency is
unable to convene a "quorum" of its members.

impossible for the decision to be made without the participation of a disqualified official, and

statute authorizing the decision." You asked if the fact that the Department could issue sub-
permits itself or negotiate with another governmental entity to hold the Master Permit would

authorizing the decision." The fact that the Department could implement its policies in other
ways does not constitute an alternate source for the decision required to be made by the SRCD or
SVRCD board concerning their role and liability under the program..

The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that
"[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests tf p".ro^
who have supported them." (Section Sl00l(b).) In furtherance of this goal, Section 8710-0 of
the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in makinglor otherwise using his or
her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 requires analysis
of the questions outlined below.
Steps One and Two: Are the individuals in question "public oflicials,, and will they be
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency. Regulation lg70l provides:
"(a) For purposes of Government Code section 82048, which defines
'public official,' and Govemment Code section 820lg,which defines "designated
employee," the following definitions apply:
"(l) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried
members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking authority. ..
You stated that both the SVRCD and SRCD Boards will be asked to make decisions
concerning the Department of Fish and Game Incidental Take Permit or the Streambed
Alteration Agreement Memorandum of Understanding and Master List of Terms and Conditions.
Thus, the members of the boards will be making decisions. Both Boards have decisionmaking
authority. Thus, all thedirectors are public officials subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).)
You have asked whether the directors can vote on the permit program. The directors will
"make a governmental decision" if they vote on the Department of Fish and Game Incidental
Take Permit or the Streambed Alteration Agreement Mimorandum of Understanding and Master
List of Terms and Conditions. (Regulations 1g702 - 19702.3.)

financial effects as effects on the personal finances of the officials.
Step Four: Are the directors' economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
Business interests and Sources of Income: Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business
entity or source of income is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency when
that business entity or source of income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A plrson is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, deniai or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
None of the businesses or sources of income in question are applicants with respect to the
decisions in question, nor would they be considered the iubject of the decision.
a Section 82005 of the Act defines "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including
but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, businesslrust, joint venture, syndicate, corpoiation or
association.
5 while it is indeed true that owners of businesses in many cases will be impacted personally if their businesses are
financially impacted (such as Director Krum needing to add capital to the business or the other directors who
operate their businesses as sole proprietors), the intent of the language in Regulation 18705.5(a) was to avoid
reanalyzing the same financial effect twice -- first under a $20,000 materiality threshold for the official's business,
and then at a $250 threshold for personal financial effects. Absent the limiting language, the lower materiality
threshold of the personal financial et'fects rule would swallow the materiality itanoiralapplicable to businesses and
real property.

Real Property: With respect to real property, Regulation 18704.2 provides:
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is
directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the governmental decision.
For purposes of subdivision (a)(5), real property is located "within 500 feet of the

from any city, county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real

real property. For puqposes of this subdivision, the terms "zoning" and
"rezoning" shall refer to the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land use
designation on the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(3) The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses
of the real property in which the official, has an interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real pioperty in
which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of thl above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of it
is located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets,

QUESTION: . Thus, the interests would all be considered indirectly involved. (Regulation 1870a(a).)

Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably
foresseeable that the standard will be met?
The next step is to identify the materiality standards that apply and whether it is
foreseeable the standard will be met. Regulation 18706 provides:
"(a) A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, within the meaning of Government code section 87103, if it is
substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards (see Cal. Code
Regs., tit. 2, $$ 18704,18705) applicable to that economic interest will be met as
a result of the governmental decision."
"[T]he question of whether financial consequences upon a business entity are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a govemmental decision is made must always depend on the facts of eaCh
particular case." (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) In each case, the official must look
at the facts surrounding each decision, and the nature of the economic interest to determine
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision is substantially likely to cause a material
financial effect as set forth above.
The Commission is not a finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Because the determination of foreseeability and materiality is necessarily a factual question, we
must leave this ultimate determination to you. If it is reasonably foreseeable that thi applicable
materiality standard for any of your economic interests will be met, then you will Ue protriUitea
from participating in the specific plan update decisions.
Business Entities: Regulation 18705.1(c) provides for a business that in the most recent
fiscal year had net income of less than $750,000 (such as the businesses of SVRCD Director
Meamber and Kuck, and the businesses of SRCD Directors Krum, Farnum, and Spencer),6 the
financial effect of a govemmental decision on the business is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
6 We did not have the financial size of the ranches of Director Barnes and Black businesses, but assume that the net
income of these businesses in the most recent fiscal year is less than $750,000. If the net income is higher, higher
thresholds apply in Regulation 18705.1(c).


Our Page 12
You noted that the associated expense of applying for a permit without the master permit
plan in place could be as high as $20,000. The Commission cannot act as a finder of fact,-but if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the directors businesses will incur or reduce expenses by more
than $5,000 in a fiscal year, the financial effect is considered material.
You stated that SRCD Director Black's business would not be subject to the program.
Thus, under the regulation the effects would not be considered material. Ao*"rer, you noted
that his businesses expenses might still increase if the inigation district that providei hi- water
has to obtain a permit and increases its rates. However, based on the limited facts before us, this
financial effect seems speculative at best. The irrigation district would have to make the
independent determination to obtain a permit, to recover some or all of the costs through rate
increases, and these rate increases would have to equal $5,000 or more on Director Black,s
business. Thus, it does not appear foreseeable that Director Black's business will be materially
affected or that he will have a conflict of interest. However, we must leave this ultimate factual
determination to you.
Nonprofits: Director Lemos has an interest in a nonprofit, the Shasta River Water
Association, as a source of income. We do not know the size of the Shasta River Water
Association. For a relatively small nonprofit, Regulation 18705.3(b)(2XF) provides:
"For an entity whose gross annual receipts are $100,000 or less, the effect
of the decision will be any of the following:
"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity's gross
annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.
"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $2,500 or more.
"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the
entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more.,,
Again, in light of your estimate of the financial expense of a permit absent the master
permit program, the financial effect on the expenses of the Association would be material.
Real Property: Regulation 18705.2(b) provides for indirectly involved real property
interests.
"The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is
indirectly involved in the govemmental decision is presumed not tobe material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that theie are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, ard the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on


Our Page 13
the real property in which the public official has an interest. Examples of specific
circumstances that will be considered include, but are not limited t;,
circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
The material financial effects of the decisions on the fair market value of the property on
which the directors operate their farms and ranches is a bit more speculative. presumabiy, the
cost of the permit can affect the amount that a buyer might pay to purchase the property. In any
case, the potential materiality of this financial effect and wtiether the pr"sumpti;n ;f n;
materiality will be rebutted is a factual determination that we must leave to you and the officials
in question.
Step Seven: "Public Generally,, Exception
You have also asked if we determine the board members have a conflict of interest under
the Act, may they nevertheless participate in the decision under the "public generally exception.,,
(Section 87103, Regulation 18707.) Regulation l87O7.l provides thl gene[l rule for appiying
the public generally exception -- the decision must affect a 1t; .igrifi.it ,"g-.nt of the i,,iUfi"
generally in (2) substantially the same manner as it affects the public official. For real property
interests, the "significant segment" is defined as l0 percent of all real property owners, or all
residential property owners, in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or ih" dirtri"t the official
represents or 5,000 real property owners, or residential property owners, in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency. For business interests, the public generaiy threshold is eveniigh er -- 25
percent or more or 2,000 business entities operating within the city.
You have indicated that the number of potential sub-permittees under the permitting
program would not meet the thresholds set forth above for a "significant segment."
You also reference the "exceptional circumstances" exception. This is an exception to
situations where, while the segment of the population affected is less than stated in the other
provisions of the regulation, due to exceptional circumstances, the population affected by the
decision is a "significant" segment and the public generally exception applies. (Regulaiion
18707.1 (b).) This provision has been inte{preted very narrowly. "This iiception was intended
for application in circumstances qualitatively different from thenorm when the decisions, for
example, might involve measures taken in response to natural disasters." (lackson Advice
Letter, No. 4-96-338.) [t was adopted in conjunction with an exception applicable to states of


Our Page 14
emergency (Regulation 18707 .6), both of which generally apply where large numbers of persons
are affected.
While the potential consequences of inaction if the board is unable to act do appear
significant, it will not be a direct effect of the disqualifications in question. Not only can
operators act individually to comply with the new rules, but the Department also has discretion
on whether to enforce or fine users that do not substantially comply with its rules. In addition,
although the permittees are part of major industries within the Distiict and within the County, 70-
75 potential permittees would still not be a sufficiently large population within the district to
constitute a significant segment under any of the provisions in Regulation 18707. Therefore, we
conclude that the "public generally" exception does not apply.
Step Eight: "Legally Required Participation,, Exception
Section 87101 allows an official, who is otherwise disqualified to participate in a
governmental decision, if the official's participation is "legally required." (Section 87101;
Regulation 18708.) This exception is narrowly construed and appiies only when ir is legally
impossible for the decision to be made without the participationof a disqualified official, and
where there is no "altemative source of decision consistent with the pu.por"r and terms of the
statute authorizing the decision." (Regulation 18708(a), (c), and (d); In-re Tobias (Iggg) L3
FPPC Ops. 5.) Typically this exception is invoked when, due to disqualification, an agency is
unable to convene a "quorum" of its members.
As noted above, this exception is narrowly construed and applies only when it is legally
impossible for the decision to be made without the participation of a disqualified official, and
where there is no "alternative source of decision consistent with the prrptr.r and terms of the
statute authorizing the decision." You asked if the fact that the Department could issue sub-
permits itself or negotiate with another govemmental entity to hold the Master permit would
constitute an "alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute
authorizing the decision." The fact that the Department could impliment its policies in other
ways does not constitute an alternate source for the decision required to be made by the SRCD or
svRCD board concerning their role and liability under the program..
Thus we conclude:
The SVRCD has five members. Three members constitute a quomm. Since we have
concluded that three of the five members have a conflict of inierest under the Act, then
the rule of legally required participation does apply to your facts since there would only
be two members left and three are needed to constitute a quorum. Accordingly, the thrie
disqualified members could participate in a random selection process to choose which
one official can participate in the decision in order to create a quorum. (In re Hudson
(1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13.)
a

a
Similarly, since the SRCD cannot achieve a quorum due to disqualification, two members
may be selected to participate in the decisions.

CONCLUSION: l. The three SVRCD (Directors Kuck, Meamber, and Lemos) and four of the five SRCD
directors (Directors Krum, Barnes, Famam, and Spencer) are disquafriea from voting to approve
the program documents if there will be a material financial effect on their economic interests, as
described below. SRCD Director Black does not appear to have a conflict of interest under your
facts.
2. The public generally exception, including the "exceptional circumstances" provision,
does not apply.
3. Assuming that either the SVRCD or SRCD cannot reach a quorum due to the conflicts
of interest, then the rule of legally required participation applies.

Krum, David A. Black, Richard G. Barnes, Greg Farnam, and John P. Please note that our advice is based solely on provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Also note our advice is based solely on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. Our File No. A-10-027 Page 2 QUESTIONS 1. Do the three SVRCD directors (Directors Kuck, Meamber, and Lemos) and five SRCD directors (Directors Krum, Black, Barnes, Farnam, and Spencer) have disqualifying conflicts of interest in voting on the Watershed-wide Permitting Program (the "program") Documents? 2. Does the "exceptional circumstances" exception in Regulation 18707 apply to allow the disqualified members to vote despite the conflict of interest? 3. If the three SVRCD and five SRCD directors are determined to be disqualified from voting, will any of the directors be legally required to participate? CONCLUSIONS 1. The three SVRCD (Directors Kuck, Meamber, and Lemos) and four of the five SRCD directors (Directors Krum, Barnes, Farnam, and Spencer) are disqualified from voting to approve the program documents if there will be a material financial effect on their economic interests, as described below. SRCD Director Black does not appear to have a conflict of interest under your facts. 2. The public generally exception, including the "exceptional circumstances" provision, does not apply. 3. Assuming that either the SVRCD or SRCD cannot reach a quorum due to the conflicts of interest, then the rule of legally required participation applies.
